narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 37 and a middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways but faced challenges in key academic areas that the admissions model weighs heavily. The model predicts a 69.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected—schools use this prediction to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in their programs. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, meaning your grades placed you in the bottom 10% of your peers that year, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack average around the 5th or 6th decile. Next in importance, your Year 1 decile of 1 similarly pulled the prediction toward failure, again showing early grades in the lowest 10% compared to most students' stronger mid-range performance. On a brighter note, your LSAT score worked strongly in your favor, nudging the prediction toward passing since it's slightly above the average of 37 for admitted students. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, while below the typical 3.21 average, and your plan to attend full-time—like over 90% of students—played smaller roles overall. Compared to typical applicants who pass the bar prediction, your consistently low early and later school deciles set you apart as a higher risk, even with that competitive LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male from a middle-income background; it's the model highlighting patterns where weaker school rankings often correlate with bar struggles. To move forward, focus on improving your academic record—perhaps retake courses or pursue a post-baccalaureate program to boost your deciles. Consider LSAT prep or additional undergrad-level work to lift your GPA closer to averages. Reach out to admissions advisors for targeted feedback, and explore schools with holistic reviews that might value your LSAT strength more. With targeted steps, you can reshape your profile for stronger future applications. Keep pushing; many overcome early hurdles to succeed in law.
